title: Online guided meditation training (Isha Kriya) improves self-reported symptoms of anxiety and depression within 2 weeks of practice-An observational study
headline: Online guided meditation training (Isha Kriya) improves self-reported symptoms of anxiety and depression withi
summary: A randomized controlled trial of Isha Kriya examined stress & anxiety. These findings suggest that daily Isha Kriya practice could alleviate symptoms of these conditions.
keyFindings: These findings suggest that daily Isha Kriya practice could alleviate symptoms of these conditions.
limitations: The changes in the self-reported anxiety and depression scores were examined by the linear mixed effect models.
Key outcomes were self-reported, which can inflate apparent effects.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (Isha Kriya) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared breath-and-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Online guided meditation training (Isha Kriya) improves self-reported symptoms of anxiety and depression withi
aiSnippet: RCT, Frontiers in psychiatry (2022). These findings suggest that daily Isha Kriya practice could alleviate symptoms of these conditions.
whyItMatters: MoM shares its breath-and-attention structure and brief, guided daily format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
